data.title: Gold and silver rack up new record highs on geopolitical tensions, softer U.S. dollar
data.description:
Gold futures closed above $4,900/oz for the first time Thursday, and silver also surged to a fresh all-time high, sparked by ‌continued geopolitical tensions, a softer U.S. dollar and expectations of rate cuts from the U.S. Federal Reserve.

The gains came even after President Trump said a "framework of a future deal" for U.S. access to Greenland was reached and that new tariffs against European Union countries would not be implemented, as the details of any agreement were unclear.

The latest report on U.S. Personal Consumption Expenditures showed rising consumer spending in November and October, indicating a third straight quarter of strong growth.

Goldman Sachs raised its year-end gold price forecast to $5,400/oz from $4,900/oz previously, citing a broadening of private sector diversification into bullion.

"We assume that private sector diversification buyers - whose purchases hedge global policy risks and have driven the upside surprise to our price forecast - don't liquidate their gold holdings in 2026, effectively lifting the starting point of our price forecast," Goldman wrote, adding that it expects central bank purchases to average 60 tons in 2026 as emerging market central banks continue diversifying reserves into gold, contributing to the price increase.

Silver also is continuing its scorching rally, establishing a new record high above $95/oz, and has a "far more compelling fundamental narrative than gold," Tradu senior analyst Nikos Tzabouras said in a note. "Maybe it's not a reserve asset in the way that gold is, but it still benefits from safe-haven flows, and ​dollar weakness."

Although the silver market remains well supported over the long term, BCA Research chief strategist Roukaya Ibrahim warned a speculative frenzy has become the main driving force behind the metal's rally and could prove unsustainable.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for January delivery closed +1.6% to $4,908.80/oz, and front-month January Comex silver (XAGUSD:CUR) settled +4.1% to $95.976/oz, both new record highs.

ETFs: (GLD), (GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: Streamex plans proposed public offering
data.description:
• Streamex (NASDAQ: STEX) announced on Thursday that it intends to offer shares of common stock in an underwritten public offering, without disclosing the size or terms of the sale.
  • The company plans to use the net proceeds to repay prior indebtedness and for working capital and general corporate purposes.
  • Needham & Company and Siebert are acting as joint book-running managers of the offering.
  • Shares +12.34%.

data.title: Legato Merger prices $200M initial public offering
data.description:
• Legato Merger (LEGO.U) on Thursday, said that it has priced its initial public offering of 20M units at $10 per unit, raising $200M, expected to begin trading on Jan. 23, 2026, and underwriters are granted a 45-day option to purchase up to 3M additional units at the IPO price.
  • Each unit consists of one ordinary share and one-third of a redeemable warrant, with each whole warrant exercisable to purchase one ordinary share at $11.50.
  • BTIG, LLC is acting as the sole book-running manager for the offering.

data.title: Albertsons prices $2.1B senior notes offering
data.description:
• Albertsons Companies (NYSE: ACI) on Thursday said that it has priced an upsized private offering of $1.2 billion of 5.625% senior notes due 2032 and $900 million of additional 5.750% senior notes due 2034.

  • The offering is expected to close on or about February 2, 2026.

  • Proceeds, together with cash on hand, will be used to redeem $1.35 billion of 4.625% senior notes due 2027 and $750 million of 5.875% senior notes due 2028, as well as to pay related fees and expenses.

  • The additional 2034 notes will be issued under the same indenture as Albertsons’ existing 5.750% senior notes due 2034.
  • ACI +0.11% after hours to $17.2.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Great Southern signals continued capital strength and margin discipline amid challenging loan growth environment
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Great Southern Bancorp, Inc. (GSBC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Joseph Turner, President and CEO, opened the call by highlighting, "Our fourth quarter and full year 2025 results reflect the sustained success of our core banking operations and our commitment to long-term tangible book value appreciation despite a volatile economic environment." Turner noted a focus on preserving net interest margin, credit quality, controlling noninterest expense, and opportunistic stock repurchases. He reported, "For the fourth quarter, we reported net income of $16.3 million or $1.45 per diluted common share compared to $14.9 million or $1.27 per diluted common share in the year ago quarter."

  • Turner added, "Net interest income for the 2025 fourth quarter totaled $49.2 million, which was a decrease of $371,000 or 0.7% compared to the prior year quarter," primarily due to the loss of income from a terminated swap and lower loan balances. Despite these factors, effective funding cost management resulted in net interest margin expansion to 3.70% from 3.49% in the year-ago quarter.

  • Turner stated, "Net loans receivable totaled $4.36 billion at year-end, representing a decline of $333.5 million or 7.1% from where they were a year ago," driven by elevated payoff activity. He emphasized, "Credit quality remains a clear area of strength at year-end. Nonperforming assets for the fourth quarter totaled $8.1 million, representing 0.15% of assets."

  • On expense management, Turner noted, "Noninterest expense for the fourth quarter of 2025 was $36 million, down about $947,000 or 2.6% from the year ago quarter."

  • Rex Copeland, Treasurer, added, "For the quarter ended December 31, 2025, we reported net income of $16.3 million or $1.45 per diluted common share compared to $14.9 million or $1.27 per diluted common share in the fourth quarter of 2024 and $17.8 million or $1.56 per diluted common share in the third quarter of 2025." Copeland highlighted, "Net interest income totaled $49.2 million for the fourth quarter of 2025 compared to $49.5 million in the prior year quarter and $50.8 million in the third quarter of 2025."

  • Copeland also focused on capital, stating, "Stockholders' equity was $636.1 million at December 31, 2025, an increase of $36.6 million from $599.6 million at the end of 2024."

Outlook

  • Turner described priorities as "maintaining strong capital and liquidity, supporting our customers and communities, maintaining strong credit metrics and deploying capital thoughtfully." He noted, "Though loan growth may remain challenging and economic conditions fluid, we believe our conservative approach and sound balance sheet management will continue delivering long-term value for our stockholders."

  • Copeland commented on margin, "We were able to bring some of our funding costs down...If there is a bias, it would be a slight bias to go maybe a little bit higher, but it's not very meaningful in the overall scope of our level of net interest income." Turner added, "I think, Damon, until the Fed takes some action, I think we will see...maybe some of that will reprice and we'll see some interest expense go down there, but that's not a very big portion of our deposits."

  • For loan growth, Turner stated, "I think it's still going to be a challenging loan growth market for us because there's just -- while there's good activity, it's not great, and there still is outsized loan portfolio or loan payoffs."

Financial Results

  • Net income for Q4 2025 was $16.3 million or $1.45 per diluted common share, compared to $17.8 million or $1.56 per diluted common share in Q3 2025.

  • Net interest income was $49.2 million for Q4 2025 versus $50.8 million in Q3 2025.

  • Noninterest income totaled $7.2 million in Q4 2025, slightly up from $7.1 million in Q3 2025 due to a $289,000 increase in late charges and fees on loans.

  • Total assets at year-end were $5.60 billion, down from $5.74 billion at September 30, 2025.

  • Noninterest expense was $36.0 million, compared to $36.1 million in Q3 2025.

  • The efficiency ratio was 63.89% in Q4 2025 versus 62.45% in Q3 2025.

  • During Q4, the company repurchased 241,000 shares of common stock at an average price of $59.33.

Q&A

  • Damon Del Monte, KBW: Questioned margin performance and expectations for 2026. Copeland responded, "We were able to bring some of our funding costs down...So it's a combination of a few of those things."

  • Del Monte asked about loan growth and payoff trends. Turner replied, "I think it's still going to be a challenging loan growth market for us...there still is outsized loan portfolio or loan payoffs."

  • Del Monte inquired on expense outlook. Turner answered, "Yes. I mean, I think that's fair." Copeland elaborated, "We do have a lot of our employee base does have annual normal increases...Payroll taxes will reset."

  • John Rodis, Janney: Asked about the potential for further loan declines. Turner replied, "It's just really hard to say, John. Obviously, I hope we've seen kind of the worst of it...But because loan repayments is such a big part of the calculation, it's just hard to kind of guarantee that one way or the other."

  • Rodis asked about the securities portfolio and reinvestment. Copeland responded, "No, we've pretty much been taking the cash flows and reinvesting in loans."

  • Rodis asked about buyback plans. Turner noted, "Yes, we like where our stock is trading at, John...we see that as a good value. And particularly while we're not growing a lot, a good use of capital."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on margin sustainability, loan growth challenges, and expense direction, reflecting a slightly negative to neutral sentiment with recurring questions on payoffs and growth headwinds.

  • Management maintained a neutral, measured tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing stability and discipline. In Q&A, both Turner and Copeland acknowledged challenges and uncertainty, often stating, "it's really hard to say" or "I don't have a lot of clarity." Confidence was strongest when discussing capital and credit quality.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment remains cautious about loan growth, while management’s tone remains steady but slightly more guarded regarding growth prospects and margin improvement.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Loan balances declined further in Q4, with management continuing to cite elevated payoffs as a headwind—this was also referenced in Q3 but appears more pronounced.

  • The efficiency ratio increased compared to Q3, reflecting slightly higher expenses relative to revenues.

  • Capital ratios and book value improved, and the company continued aggressive share repurchases.

  • Management’s confidence in credit quality and capital strength remains intact, while forecasts for loan growth and margin improvement remain cautious.

  • Analyst questions focused on growth outlook, margin sustainability, and capital deployment, similar to Q3 but with more emphasis on payoffs and future loan trends.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified continued elevated loan payoffs as a key challenge, complicating loan growth forecasts.

  • Deposit competition remains intense, with ongoing focus on balancing rate discipline with customer retention.

  • Expense pressures may rise in 2026 due to annual increases in salaries and payroll taxes.

  • Management reiterated a conservative approach, emphasizing credit discipline and balance sheet strength as mitigation strategies.

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized that despite ongoing challenges from elevated loan payoffs and a highly competitive deposit environment, Great Southern Bancorp remains committed to disciplined margin management, strong credit quality, and capital strength. The company will continue to pursue share repurchases and maintain a balanced approach to loan growth while prioritizing prudent underwriting and cost control. Management sees its capital position and asset quality as key pillars for long-term shareholder value, even as near-term growth may remain constrained by external market dynamics.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Lsi Industries signals continued growth into fiscal 2027 as Lighting orders rise 10% and backlog improves
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: LSI Industries Inc. (LYTS) Q2 2026

Management View

  • CEO James Clark reported a solid second quarter, with revenue flat year-over-year at $147 million, and highlighted improved profitability and free cash flow. Clark stated the company "delivered a solid second quarter with results that were in line with our expectations." He noted that Lighting achieved sales growth of 15% year-over-year, driven by large project shipments, new product introductions, and the addition of aluminum poles to the steel pole line. Clark emphasized, "As we exit the second quarter, Lighting orders were up approximately 10% year-over-year, resulting in book-to-bill above 1."
  • Clark detailed Display Solutions' performance, explaining that while revenues declined slightly due to tough prior year comparisons, orders improved both sequentially and year-over-year, leading to a stronger backlog entering Q3. He identified growing traction in the casual dining and premium food services markets with projects ranging from $250,000 to $1 million per location, stating, "These opportunities align well with our capabilities in custom fabrication, integrated design and program execution."
  • Clark highlighted the ongoing integration of JSI and EMI, stressing the importance of talent management, role design, and cross-team alignment to unlock cross-selling opportunities. He also announced the hiring of a senior sales leader for Display Solutions to enhance sales pipeline visibility and cross-brand alignment.
  • CFO James Galeese noted, "Adjusted earnings per share were $0.26 for the quarter. Cash flow in the quarter was higher than expected, over $23 million, following a timing-related softer first quarter. The strong cash flow lowered our debt-to-EBITDA leverage ratio to 0.4x, providing significant capital allocation flexibility."

Outlook

  • Clark projected continued progress into the second half of fiscal '26, supported by improving order trends and backlog. He remarked, "We remain confident in the secular growth outlook across our key vertical markets and in our ability to grow above market through differentiated solutions-based approach."
  • Galeese stated, "We expect the favorable momentum to continue into the second half of fiscal '26 as Lighting orders for Q2 were 10% above prior year, a book-to-bill ratio above 1 on strong shipment quarter and an improved backlog."

Financial Results

  • The company reported sales of $147 million in Q2. Adjusted EBITDA reached $13.4 million, with margin performance benefiting from disciplined project pricing, productivity improvements, and cost management. Free cash flow was $23 million, and debt was reduced by $22.7 million during the quarter. The net leverage ratio ended at 0.4.
  • Lighting segment saw its third consecutive quarter of double-digit growth, with sales up 15% and adjusted operating income increasing 29%. Adjusted gross margin for Lighting improved by 190 basis points year-over-year.
  • Display Solutions experienced normalized grocery demand and lower sales compared to last year, but adjusted gross margin improved by 30 basis points on lower volume. Grocery orders rose double digits year-over-year, with a book-to-bill of 1.2 and a building backlog. Activity in the refueling/c-store vertical remained high with new midsized projects contributing to a sustainable repeat business model.

Q&A

  • Aaron Spychalla, Craig-Hallum: Asked about refueling/c-store growth and market consistency. Clark replied, "We have a number of those projects, smaller in scale, but the same kind of makeup where we have...a nice kind of pathway in front of us going to bring us through the remainder of '26 and into '27."
  • Spychalla inquired about Mexico's market drivers and pipeline. Clark indicated the environment has normalized and "activity is on an upswing," with Galeese adding, "In the intermediate term, we see that upswing looking positive to continue."
  • Spychalla asked about EMI integration and margin progress. Clark responded, "They've had better than 200 bps of improvement in their [indiscernible] margin. And we're continuing on that."
  • Christopher Glynn, Oppenheimer: Sought details on premium food services and campus opportunities. Clark explained, "The number of locations tends to be smaller...but the scale of the project is much bigger. 10, 20x the size of our smaller projects." Glynn asked about leverage ratio targets, with Clark stating comfort below 3 and a current leverage ratio of 0.4.
  • Alexander Rygiel, Texas Capital: Requested updates on Canada's Best integration and U.S. banking entry, with Clark reporting progress and ongoing collaboration efforts. Rygiel also asked about pricing strategy, with Clark emphasizing disciplined price adjustments and flexibility in project negotiations. Rygiel inquired about CapEx needs, with Clark responding, "Our CapEx is relatively small. We don't see anything material impacting that at least in the foreseeable future."
  • George Gianarikas, Canaccord: Questioned above-market growth conviction and M&A environment. Clark cited disruption and investment in verticals, stating, "I feel like we just have an opportunity to continue to win and not only win those projects, but accelerate our win rate." On M&A, Clark said, "The multiples are more realistic. The conversations are more business-oriented."
  • Sameer Joshi, H.C. Wainwright: Probed visibility and profitability in casual dining projects, with Clark stating larger project sizes and faster cross-selling but limited current visibility, "If you give us 2, 3 more quarters to talk about it, I'll have more visibility and maybe a stronger story to tell." Joshi also asked about Q3 Display segment seasonality, with Clark expecting performance to track historical patterns but outperform the prior year.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed optimism about growth opportunities, while seeking clarity on market consistency, new verticals, and integration progress. The tone was constructive, with incremental probing on international prospects and margin advancements.
  • Management maintained a confident and positive tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently emphasizing momentum, execution, and market opportunities. Clark repeatedly cited "confidence," "enthusiasm," and strong team execution. The tone was consistent with the previous quarter, though with more emphasis on integration and growth in premium verticals.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter featured greater focus on integration, cross-selling, and expansion into premium food services and international markets compared to Q1, which concentrated on double-digit growth in both Display Solutions and Lighting. Both calls discussed normalization in grocery demand and the impact of prior year event-driven spikes.
  • Management sustained an optimistic tone, now highlighting increased backlog, margin expansion, and improved free cash flow. Analysts continued to focus on growth drivers, operational integration, pricing, and new verticals.
  • Key metrics shifted from double-digit revenue growth in Q1 to flat sales but stronger profitability and cash flow in Q2. Lighting maintained robust growth, while Display Solutions shifted from high growth to normalized patterns with improved backlog.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted ongoing cost inflation as a challenge, with mitigation through disciplined project pricing, productivity, and cost management.
  • Clark referenced uncertainty in the timing of investments in the QSR vertical, stating, "timing of release remains unclear."
  • Rygiel raised questions on CapEx and operational improvements, which management indicated were not material in the near term.
  • Management acknowledged global trade disruptions in Mexico but observed that conditions had normalized and activity was rebounding.

Final Takeaway

LSI Industries closed the second quarter of fiscal 2026 with flat revenue but strengthened profitability, increased free cash flow, and a significantly reduced leverage ratio. Management emphasized strong order trends in Lighting, growing opportunities in premium food services and international markets, and a continued focus on operational integration and cross-selling. The company signaled confidence in above-market growth, supported by a robust backlog and ongoing investments in people and process improvements.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Praetorian prices $220M initial public offering
data.description:
• Praetorian Acquisition (PTORU) said on Thursday it has priced its initial public offering of 22M  at $10 per unit, raising $220M, with the shares expected to begin trading on Jan. 23, 2026, with underwriters granted a 45-day option to purchase up to 3.3M additional units.
  • Each unit consists of one Class A ordinary share and one-third of a redeemable warrant, with each whole warrant exercisable to purchase one Class A ordinary share at $11.50.
  • No fractional warrants will be issued upon unit separation, and only whole warrants will trade.
  • The company is led by CEO Justin Di Rezze and CFO Peter Ondishin, both members of the Board, which also includes Nicole Seligman, Alex Elias, and Erica Dorfman.
  • The offering is expected to close on January 26, 2026.

data.title: Capital Southwest launches first-out senior loan joint venture
data.description:
Capital Southwest (CSWC)  formed a joint venture with another private credit asset manager to invest primarily in first-out senior secured debt opportunities in the lower middle-market, the company said on Thursday.

The joint venture will be owned equally by Capital Southwest (CSWC) and its joint venture partner, with each holding a 50% equity interest. All investment and operational decisions for the fund will be made by the joint venture’s board of managers, which will consist of equal representation from both joint venture partners.

The company expects the joint venture will fund investments through a senior secured credit facility it's expected to enter.

"We believe the creation of this joint venture will enhance CSWC’s ability to compete for and win high‑quality lower middle market opportunities by providing more flexible capital solutions," said CSWC CEO Michael Sarner. "It also will allow CSWC to allocate portions of larger transactions to the joint venture, enabling us to maintain portfolio granularity while broadening the range of platform companies we can pursue in the lower middle market."

Capital Southwest (CSWC) stock rose 0.7% in after-hours trading.

data.title: Western Exploration prices C$8M brokered bought-deal offering
data.description:
• Western Exploration (WEX:CA) announced on Thursday that it has agreed with Paradigm Capital, acting as lead agent and sole bookrunner, on a C$8M private placement under the LIFE exemption.
  • The offering comprises 8.7M units priced at C$0.92 each, for gross proceeds of about C$8.0M. 
  • Each unit will consist of one variable voting share and one-half of one share purchase warrant, with each whole warrant exercisable to acquire one variable voting share at C$1.35 within 24 months of the closing date.
  • Warrants become callable if the stock’s 20-day volume-weighted average exceeds C$1.70 at any time starting six months after closing and before warrant expiry.
  • The agents have been granted an option to sell up to an additional 1.08M units at the offering price for additional gross proceeds of up to C$0.93M. 
  • Net proceeds will be used to fund exploration and advancement of its Aura project in Nevada, as well as for general corporate purposes and working capital.
  • The offering is scheduled to close on February 3, 2026.

data.title: Pinnacle Financial targets 9%–11% loan growth and $5–5.2B revenue in 2026 as Synovus merger integration accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Pinnacle Financial Partners, Inc. (PNFP) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Kevin Blair, President and CEO, emphasized the newly combined company's commitment to "producing strong above peer revenue, earnings per share and tangible book value growth." He highlighted that the focus remains on exceptional client service and a collaborative culture, stating, "These priorities enable us to attract and retain revenue producers at an outsized pace, fueling our continued growth."
  • Blair detailed the successful merger with Synovus, completed January 1, only 160 days after announcement, noting, "Both organizations have successfully completed key milestones." He described swift integration efforts, including incorporating legacy Synovus team members into Pinnacle’s operating rhythm and sales meetings.
  • The company's 2025 performance was strong, with legacy Pinnacle growing adjusted diluted EPS by 22% and legacy Synovus by 28%. Blair said, "Our team is fully engaged, focused on our clients and delivering meaningful value for our shareholders."
  • Andrew Gregory, Executive VP & CFO, reported, "Pinnacle reported fourth quarter adjusted EPS of $2.24, which was stable quarter-over-quarter and up 18% from the prior year. Net interest income increased 3% from the third quarter and 12% year-over-year."
  • Gregory also noted, "Net charge-offs were contained at $27 million or 28 basis points, 63% of which was from a single non-owner-occupied CRE loan. The CET1 ratio ended the quarter at 10.88%."

Outlook

  • Blair projected, "We expect our period-end loans to grow to $91 billion to $93 billion or up 9% to 11% versus our combined loans at year-end 2025."
  • Total deposits are expected to reach $106.5 billion to $108.5 billion, up 8% to 10% in 2026.
  • The adjusted revenue outlook for 2026 is $5 billion to $5.2 billion. Blair stated, "The net interest margin is estimated in the 3.45% to 3.55% range."
  • Adjusted non-interest revenue is anticipated at $1.1 billion, with BHG investment income contributing approximately $125 million to $135 million.
  • Adjusted non-interest expense is forecasted at $2.7 billion to $2.8 billion in 2026, with a target of realizing 40% or $100 million of annualized merger-related expense savings.
  • Net charge-offs are estimated in the range of 20 to 25 basis points for the year. The CET1 ratio is targeted at 10.25% to 10.75%. The quarterly common equity dividend will be $0.50 per share, and a $400 million share repurchase program has been authorized.

Financial Results

  • Net interest income increased 3% from the third quarter and 12% year-over-year for Pinnacle.
  • Period-end loans grew 3% from the prior quarter and 10% year-over-year. Core deposit growth was also 3% quarter-over-quarter and 10% year-over-year.
  • The net interest margin increased to 3.27%. Adjusted non-interest revenue declined 6% from the third quarter but rose 25% year-over-year. BHG contributed $31 million in fee revenue for Pinnacle.
  • Synovus reported fourth quarter adjusted diluted EPS of $1.45, stable quarter-over-quarter and up 16% year-over-year. Net interest income increased 2% quarter-over-quarter and 7% year-over-year, with period-end loan growth of $872 million, or 2% from the prior quarter.
  • Synovus’ net interest margin reached 3.45% and adjusted non-interest revenue grew 6% from the prior quarter and 16% year-over-year.
  • Both firms hired 41 new revenue producers in the fourth quarter, totaling 217 for 2025.

Q&A

  • Ebrahim Poonawala, BofA Securities, asked about merger systems conversion and new banker onboarding. Blair responded that both companies are operating on legacy platforms until the 2027 conversion, but "it's not stopping our ability to grow the business."
  • Poonawala followed up about the $400 million buyback. Gregory answered, "It's unlikely you would see them in the second quarter, but then we will reassess as we get into -- later into the year."
  • John Pancari, Evercore, inquired about loan growth confidence. Blair cited, "we generated 10% loan growth already" in Q4 and expressed confidence in achieving 9% to 11% due to hiring and specialty business expansion.
  • Pancari asked about cost saves; Gregory explained, "those 2 things really drove the 50% down to 40% on the year 1 cost saves...it's really a timing difference."
  • Jared Shaw, Barclays, asked about fee income guidance for Capital Markets. Gregory highlighted "over 10% growth in account analysis fees...over 15% growth in swap fees," with expectations for double-digit growth in 2026.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts exhibited a constructive but probing tone, focusing on merger integration, growth outlook, cost saves, and competitive positioning.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently using phrases such as "we are confident" and "I am more excited than ever."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone has shifted to emphasize operational integration and future growth, while analyst sentiment remained attentive but slightly more focused on execution risk.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Current quarter brought detailed post-merger integration updates and explicit forward guidance for the combined entity, compared to the prior quarter’s focus on standalone results and merger anticipation.
  • Loan growth guidance increased from 9% to 10% (previous quarter) to 9% to 11% (current quarter) for the combined firm.
  • Share repurchase and dividend policies were outlined more explicitly in the current quarter.
  • Analysts shifted from merger mechanics and hiring momentum to integration execution, cost synergies, and specialty business growth.
  • Management confidence remained high, but now with a greater emphasis on merger execution and delivering on promised synergies.

Risks and Concerns

  • Blair acknowledged "ongoing challenges, especially as we prepare for conversion in the first quarter of 2027," but emphasized readiness.
  • Analysts queried the timing and realization of cost synergies, with management attributing delays to rapid deal closure and system integration timing.
  • Credit risk was discussed, with net charge-offs mainly attributed to a single CRE loan, and guidance for stability in charge-off levels.
  • The outlook incorporates potential headwinds from economic uncertainty and competitive hiring/retention dynamics.

Final Takeaway

Management highlighted that 2026 marks the first full year as a combined company, with clear targets for loan and deposit growth, merger-related expense savings, and robust revenue expansion. The successful integration with Synovus, continued strong hiring momentum, and a focus on operational excellence position Pinnacle Financial to achieve its growth and profitability objectives, while remaining attentive to integration, credit, and competitive risks in the evolving market landscape.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
